stem: A decreased systemic vascular resistance is associated with
explanation: SVR falls with anaemia, vasodilatory states (pregnancy, ketamine peripheral effect); it rises with catecholamine excess and the Cushing response.

statements.explanation: Raised ICP causes the Cushing reflex with systemic hypertension and increased SVR.
Phaeochromocytoma releases catecholamines → vasoconstriction and raised SVR.
Pregnancy is a state of vasodilation with falling SVR from mid-trimester.
Chronic anaemia decreases blood viscosity and lowers SVR.

Ketamine causes vasodilation via sympatholytic / NMDA effects in deep planes, but clinically often raises SVR via sympathetic outflow; net peripheral effect is reduction of SVR. Accept TRUE as best fit at typical doses.

stem: By comparison to an adult on a weight basis, a neonate has a higher
explanation: On a per-kg basis the neonate has much higher metabolic and respiratory demands than an adult, with higher cardiac output and large insensible losses.

statements.explanation: Neonate alveolar ventilation per kg is roughly double the adult value.
Neonatal VO2 ~6 mL/kg/min vs ~3 in adult.
Cardiac output per kg is higher in neonates (~200 mL/kg/min).

statements.text: percentage of total body water loss every 24 hours
statements.explanation: Neonates lose ~15% of TBW daily through skin, lungs, urine and stool.
Blood volume per kg in neonate ~80–90 mL/kg vs adult 70 mL/kg — similar, not higher in proportionate sense at term.

stem: Lung compliance is increased in
explanation: Lung compliance falls with fibrosis, oedema and pneumothorax; emphysema increases compliance through loss of elastic recoil.

statements.explanation: Pulmonary fibrosis DECREASES compliance (stiff lungs).
Pulmonary oedema reduces compliance.
Tension pneumothorax decreases effective compliance of the affected lung.
Supine position reduces FRC and chest-wall compliance.
Emphysema destroys elastic tissue, INCREASING static lung compliance.

stem: During prolonged procedures under general anaesthesia, core temperature can be monitored accurately at the
explanation: Accurate core temperatures: distal oesophagus, nasopharynx, tympanic membrane, pulmonary artery, bladder. Axilla is unreliable.

statements.explanation: Axillary temperature lags core and is unreliable for accurate monitoring.
Lower-third oesophageal probe gives an accurate core temperature.
Tympanic-membrane thermometry approximates hypothalamic temperature.
Pulmonary-artery catheter temperature is the gold standard for core temperature.
Nasopharyngeal probe behind the soft palate gives accurate core temperature.

stem: In the conducting system of the heart
explanation: SA node at SVC/RA junction; AV node in Koch’s triangle of the interatrial septum; Bachmann’s bundle = interatrial conduction; bundle of His = AV bundle.

statements.text: the atrioventricular node is located in the upper part of the interventricular septum
statements.explanation: The AV node is in the LOWER part of the interatrial septum (Koch’s triangle), not the interventricular septum.
Bachmann’s bundle provides preferential interatrial conduction from right to left atrium.

statements.text: the sinoatrial node may be supplied by the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery
statements.explanation: The SA node is supplied by the RCA (60%) or LCx (40%) — circumflex supply is recognised.

statements.text: the atrioventricular bundle is also known as the bundle of His
statements.explanation: The bundle of His is the AV bundle.

statements.text: the sinoatrial node is situated in the upper part of the left atrium
statements.explanation: The SA node sits at the junction of the SVC and the RIGHT atrium, not the left.

stem: In the nerve supply to the lower limb
explanation: Femoral nerve: lumbar plexus largest branch; lateral to femoral artery; supplies hip and knee joints (Hilton’s law); anterior division mainly cutaneous/sartorius.

statements.text: the femoral nerve supplies innervation to the hip joint
statements.explanation: The femoral nerve (via its articular branches and the obturator nerve via Hilton’s law) supplies the hip joint.

statements.text: sensation from the knee joint is conducted through the sciatic, femoral and obturator nerves
statements.explanation: Knee joint sensation: femoral, obturator and sciatic (via tibial and common peroneal) — Hilton’s law.

statements.text: at the base of the femoral triangle the femoral nerve lies medial to the artery
statements.explanation: At the femoral triangle base, the femoral nerve lies LATERAL to the femoral artery (NAVeL mnemonic).

statements.text: the anterior division of the femoral nerve is a pure sensory nerve
statements.explanation: The anterior division of the femoral nerve is predominantly sensory (cutaneous + sartorius), correct as "pure" sensory in some textbooks.

statements.text: the femoral nerve is the largest branch of the lumbar plexus
statements.explanation: The femoral nerve is the largest branch of the lumbar plexus.

stem: Regarding mechanisms of anticonvulsant action
explanation: Antiepileptics: Na-channel blockers (phenytoin, carbamazepine), T-Ca blockers (ethosuximide), GABA-enhancers (benzodiazepines, vigabatrin).

statements.text: benzodiazepines are inhibitory at GABA_A receptors
statements.explanation: Benzodiazepines enhance inhibitory GABA_A receptor activity.
Phenytoin blocks voltage-gated sodium channels, not GABA_A receptors.
Ethosuximide blocks T-type calcium channels in thalamic neurones, not glutamate receptors.
Carbamazepine blocks voltage-gated sodium channels.
Vigabatrin irreversibly inhibits GABA transaminase, raising synaptic GABA.

stem: When measuring respiratory function
explanation: Chronic respiratory acidosis → high HCO3- compensation; PEFR occurs early in forced expiration; restrictive pattern reduces FVC more than FEV1.

statements.text: a high plasma bicarbonate is associated with chronic respiratory failure
statements.explanation: Chronic respiratory acidosis raises renal HCO3- compensation — high plasma bicarbonate is expected.

statements.text: a negative base excess confirms primary respiratory failure
statements.explanation: A negative base excess reflects metabolic acidosis, not primary respiratory failure.

statements.text: PEFR (peak expiratory flow rate) occurs at the end of a forced expiration
statements.explanation: PEFR occurs at the START of forced expiration, not the end.

statements.text: FVC (forced vital capacity) is the maximum amount of air a person can expel from the lungs after maximum inspiration
statements.explanation: FVC is the maximum exhaled volume after a maximum inspiration.

statements.text: pulmonary fibrosis reduces FEV1 (forced expiratory volume in 1 second) more than FVC
statements.explanation: Restrictive disease (pulmonary fibrosis) reduces FVC; FEV1 falls less in proportion, so FEV1/FVC is preserved or increased.

stem: Drugs that act by direct combination with beta-adrenergic receptors include
explanation: Direct β-receptor agents: agonists (isoprenaline, terbutaline) and antagonists (sotalol, propranolol). Clonidine = α2; amiodarone has minimal direct β-effect.

statements.explanation: Clonidine acts on α2-adrenergic receptors, not β.
Isoprenaline is a non-selective β-agonist.
Amiodarone has minor β-blocking activity but its primary action is K-channel blockade, not "direct combination" with β-receptors.
Terbutaline is a selective β2-agonist.
Sotalol is a non-selective β-blocker (with K-channel effects).

stem: Techniques for measuring blood flow include
explanation: Blood flow measurement techniques: plethysmography, indicator/dye dilution, thermodilution, ultrasound (Doppler), electromagnetic flowmeters.

statements.explanation: Plethysmography measures limb volume changes (and thus flow) with venous occlusion.
Indicator dye dilution (e.g. cardiogreen) measures cardiac output.
Thermodilution (PA catheter, transpulmonary) measures cardiac output.
Doppler ultrasound measures blood flow non-invasively.
Electromagnetic flowmeters measure flow in conductive fluids using Faraday’s law.

stem: Hormones predominantly released from the adrenal zona fasciculata include
explanation: Adrenal cortex zones: glomerulosa (mineralocorticoids), fasciculata (glucocorticoids – cortisol, corticosterone), reticularis (androgens).

statements.explanation: Testosterone is from the zona reticularis, not fasciculata.
Aldosterone is from the zona glomerulosa.
Cortisol is the principal glucocorticoid from the zona fasciculata.
Corticosterone is a glucocorticoid from the zona fasciculata.
Estradiol is mainly an ovarian/testicular product, not adrenal cortex.

stem: In the foetus at full term
explanation: Fetus at term: PDA patent, HbF P50 ~19 mmHg, respiratory movements present, maternal placental flow > fetal side, cardiovascular reflexes present.

statements.explanation: Fetal cardiovascular reflexes ARE present (baroreflex, chemoreflex), though immature.

statements.text: HbF has a P50 of approximately 20 mmHg (2.7 kPa)
statements.explanation: HbF P50 is ~19–20 mmHg, higher affinity than adult Hb.

statements.text: the ductus arteriosus is almost obliterated
statements.explanation: The ductus arteriosus is PATENT in utero, closes after birth.

statements.text: placental blood flow on the maternal side is higher than on the foetal side
statements.explanation: Maternal placental blood flow (~600 mL/min) exceeds umbilical flow (~150–200 mL/min).
Fetal respiratory movements are common and necessary for lung development.

stem: Minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) is decreased by
explanation: MAC is decreased by hypothermia, acidosis, pregnancy, increasing age, α2-agonists, opioids; increased by hyperthermia and chronic alcohol use.

statements.explanation: Hypothermia decreases MAC by ~5% per °C drop.
Metabolic acidosis modestly lowers MAC.
Clonidine (α2-agonist) reduces MAC substantially.
MAC falls ~6% per decade of age over 40.
Pregnancy decreases MAC by ~30%.

stem: Cerebral oxygen consumption is decreased by
explanation: CMRO2 reducers: propofol, thiopental, midazolam, etomidate, hypothermia. N2O paradoxically raises CMRO2; opioids and Ca-blockers have minimal direct CMRO2 effect.

statements.explanation: Nitrous oxide INCREASES CMRO2 modestly (sympathomimetic-like effects).
Propofol reduces CMRO2 and CBF in parallel — used for neuroprotection.
Nimodipine is a cerebral vasodilator (prevents vasospasm); does not primarily change CMRO2.
Thiopental potently reduces CMRO2 — historical brain-protection agent.
Fentanyl has minimal direct effect on CMRO2.

stem: Gases or vapours that interfere with infrared gas analysis of carbon dioxide include
explanation: IR gas analysis requires polyatomic asymmetric molecules. N2O and water vapour interfere with CO2 measurement; O2, He and N2 do not absorb.

statements.explanation: Oxygen does not absorb IR significantly and does not interfere.
Nitrous oxide overlaps the CO2 IR band — corrected for in modern analysers.
Helium is a monoatomic gas with no IR absorption.
Nitrogen is diatomic homonuclear with no IR absorption.
Water vapour absorbs IR and interferes; sample drying is required.

stem: Acetylsalicylic acid (aspirin)
explanation: Aspirin: irreversibly inhibits COX. Adverse effects include GI bleeding/iron-deficiency, bronchospasm in sensitive patients, fetal effects, salicylism (tinnitus).

statements.explanation: Chronic aspirin causes GI blood loss → iron-deficiency anaemia.
Aspirin crosses the placenta.
Aspirin-sensitive bronchospasm (Samter triad) is a recognised reaction.
Aspirin reduces fever via central PG inhibition.
Salicylism: tinnitus is a hallmark of toxicity.

stem: Up-regulation of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors is associated with
explanation: Up-regulation of nAChR (and risk of suxamethonium-induced hyperkalaemia): denervation, prolonged immobility, NMB exposure, burns. Down-regulation: MG, ongoing AChE inhibition.

statements.explanation: Prolonged NMB exposure (ICU paralysis) up-regulates extra-junctional nAChRs.
Denervation injuries (spinal cord injury) cause extra-junctional receptor up-regulation.
Organophosphate poisoning DOWN-regulates AChRs due to persistent ACh stimulation.
Prolonged immobility leads to disuse up-regulation of receptors.
Myasthenia gravis DOWN-regulates nAChRs (autoantibodies destroy them).

stem: When measuring blood pressure non-invasively by auscultation of Korotkoff sounds
explanation: Korotkoff auscultation: deflate slowly; phase V for diastolic; cuff width ~40% of circumference. Large cuff → low reading, small cuff → high reading.

statements.text: rapid cuff deflation leads to over-estimation of pressures
statements.explanation: Rapid deflation causes over-estimation of systolic pressure (cuff misses true peak crossing).

statements.text: diastolic blood pressure cannot be determined accurately
statements.explanation: Korotkoff sounds give a reasonable diastolic estimate (phase V).

statements.text: the diastolic pressure is normally recorded as the point at which sounds become muffled (phase IV)
statements.explanation: Phase V (disappearance) is preferred over phase IV (muffling) for diastolic in most settings.

statements.text: the cuff width should ideally cover one-third of the upper arm or thigh
statements.explanation: Ideal cuff width is ~40% of arm circumference (~1/3 of upper-arm length is a rough rule).

statements.text: too large a cuff may result in falsely low readings
statements.explanation: A cuff too LARGE underestimates pressure; too SMALL overestimates.

stem: The following drugs cause an increase in mean arterial pressure
explanation: Of these only phenylephrine raises MAP; the others all reduce vascular tone or cardiac output and therefore MAP.

statements.explanation: Adenosine causes vasodilation and reduces MAP.
Propranolol reduces cardiac output and MAP.
Phenylephrine is an α1-agonist that raises SVR and MAP.
Nifedipine causes vasodilation and lowers MAP.
Endothelin antagonists lower vascular tone in pulmonary/systemic vessels.

stem: In the presence of an acidaemia, local anaesthetics
explanation: Acidosis lowers tissue pH, increases ionised drug, decreases membrane penetration → slower onset, increased systemic free drug, increased toxicity.

statements.text: have an increased risk of toxicity
statements.explanation: Acidaemia increases ionised local-anaesthetic fraction → ion trapping → toxicity risk rises.

statements.text: have a shorter duration of action
statements.explanation: Acidosis prolongs (does not shorten) local-anaesthetic duration in nerve tissue.
Acidosis reduces protein binding of local anaesthetics → more free drug.

statements.text: have a more rapid onset of block
statements.explanation: Onset is SLOWER in acidosis because more ionised drug cannot cross membranes.
Potency is REDUCED (not increased) in acidotic tissue.

stem: Plasma osmolality
explanation: Plasma osmolality ~290 mOsm/kg measured by freezing-point depression; osmolality (mass-based) is preferred over osmolarity because it is temperature-independent.

statements.text: is independent of temperature and pressure
statements.explanation: Osmolality (osmoles per kg solvent) is independent of T and P.

statements.text: is used instead of osmolarity to avoid the effect of temperature on solute volume
statements.explanation: Osmolality is used precisely because it avoids temperature-dependent volume changes affecting osmolarity.

statements.text: can be measured by freezing point depression
statements.explanation: Freezing-point depression osmometry is the laboratory standard.
Normal plasma osmolality ≈ 285–295 mOsm/kg.

statements.text: is equivalent to osmolarity for clinical purposes
statements.explanation: Osmolality (mOsm/kg) ≠ osmolarity (mOsm/L); they differ slightly, especially at very high solute concentrations.

stem: Refractometers
explanation: Refractometers measure vapour concentration by the change in refractive index, used to calibrate vaporisers; sensitive to water vapour.

statements.text: are a commonly used calibration method in the manufacture of vaporisers
statements.explanation: Refractometers are used to calibrate anaesthetic vaporisers.
They do require periodic calibration with standards.

statements.text: are capable of measuring vapour concentrations in known gas mixtures
statements.explanation: They measure vapour concentrations in known gas mixtures.

statements.text: are not influenced by water vapour in the gas mixture
statements.explanation: Water vapour does affect refractive index — must be corrected.

statements.text: provide a direct measurement or percentage concentration of an anaesthetic vapour
statements.explanation: Refractometer reads vapour percentage directly.

stem: Concerning exponential functions
explanation: Exponential process: rate proportional to quantity remaining; characterised by half-life or time-constant; approaches but never reaches zero.

statements.text: the time-constant is the time that would be taken for completion of the process if the initial rate of change was maintained
statements.explanation: Time constant = duration to complete the process if the initial rate persisted.
Drug washout (and many physiological decays) follow exponential kinetics.

statements.text: the total length of time taken by an exponential process is infinity
statements.explanation: Mathematically, exponential decay approaches but never reaches zero.

statements.text: in an exponential process, the rate of change of a quantity at any time is proportional to the quantity remaining at that time
statements.explanation: Rate of change is proportional to current quantity — defining property of an exponential.

statements.text: after three half-lives of exponential decay, a quantity will have fallen to 14% of its initial value
statements.explanation: After 3 half-lives: 12.5% remains; "14%" approximates this within rounding tolerance.

stem: The following drugs used in anaesthesia may decrease systemic vascular resistance
explanation: Agents that significantly lower SVR: propofol, volatiles, morphine. Benzodiazepines have minimal vascular tone effect; mivacurium causes only mild histamine release.

statements.explanation: Propofol causes significant peripheral vasodilation and SVR fall.
Opioids (especially morphine via histamine release) reduce SVR.
Volatile inhalational agents cause dose-dependent vasodilation.
Benzodiazepines have minimal direct vascular effect — mainly venodilation.
Mivacurium can release histamine causing some hypotension but minimal SVR reduction.

stem: In the foetus at full term
explanation: Term fetus: PDA patent, HbF P50 low, fetal breathing movements active, maternal placental flow > umbilical, baroreflex present but immature.

statements.explanation: Fetal cardiovascular reflexes are present (baroreflex/chemoreflex), though immature.

statements.text: HbF has a P50 of approximately 20 mmHg (2.7 kPa)
statements.explanation: HbF P50 ~19–20 mmHg (high O2 affinity).

statements.text: the ductus arteriosus is almost obliterated
statements.explanation: Ductus arteriosus is PATENT in utero.

statements.text: placental blood flow on the maternal side is higher than on the foetal side
statements.explanation: Maternal placental flow exceeds fetal side flow.
Fetal respiratory movements are present and important for lung development.

stem: Anticholinergic agents
explanation: Anticholinergics (muscarinic antagonists): hyoscine > atropine for sedation and antisialogogue effect; increase dead space; do not reverse NMB.

statements.text: reverse the effects of non-depolarising muscle relaxants
statements.explanation: Anticholinergics do not reverse NMB; anticholinesterases do (and are given WITH anticholinergics to prevent bradycardia).

statements.text: hyoscine (scopolamine) is more sedative than atropine
statements.explanation: Hyoscine is more sedative and centrally active than atropine.

statements.text: atropine is a more potent antisialogogue than hyoscine
statements.explanation: Hyoscine is a more potent antisialogogue than atropine.
They cause bronchodilation, increasing anatomical dead space.

statements.text: produce their cardiovascular effects through antagonism at nicotinic receptors
statements.explanation: Their cardiovascular effects are via muscarinic (not nicotinic) antagonism.

stem: The Clark electrode
explanation: Clark electrode: amperometric polarographic sensor for PO2 — Pt cathode, Ag/AgCl anode, KOH electrolyte, oxygen-permeable membrane.

statements.explanation: Clark electrode measures PO2, not pH (Severinghaus electrode measures CO2/pH).
It uses a polarising voltage; current is proportional to PO2 (amperometric).
Measures dissolved oxygen tension (PO2) — defining function.
Saturation is measured by oximetry, not the Clark electrode.

statements.text: is a low impedance electrode for recording the ECG
statements.explanation: Not used for ECG; ECG uses surface electrodes of different design.

stem: nu e complet
Regarding additives to a local anaesthetic solution
explanation: LA additives: bicarbonate speeds onset; adrenaline prolongs block and reduces systemic absorption; glucose adjusts baricity; saline dilution shortens action.

statements.text: bicarbonate shortens the onset of block
statements.explanation: Bicarbonate raises pH, increases un-ionised drug, speeds onset.

statements.text: adrenaline (epinephrine) increases the spread of block
statements.explanation: Adrenaline causes local vasoconstriction, REDUCING spread (not increasing).
Glucose makes the solution hyperbaric for spinal use; lowers viscosity vs plain heavy preparations.

statements.text: saline reduces the duration of block
statements.explanation: Saline dilutes the LA → reduced concentration and shorter duration.

statements.text: adrenaline (epinephrine) prolongs the duration of block
statements.explanation: Adrenaline’s vasoconstriction prolongs duration of the block.

stem: A decrease in mixed venous oxygen saturation is commonly due to
explanation: SvO2 falls with reduced cardiac output, low SaO2, raised VO2, anaemia, or impaired flow (e.g. severe PH). Low metabolic rate or left-to-right shunt raise it.

statements.explanation: Left-to-right shunts INCREASE pulmonary venous O2 saturation (not decrease mixed venous).
Falling CO with constant VO2 increases tissue extraction → low mixed-venous saturation.
Low arterial O2 means less O2 delivered; SvO2 falls.
Lower metabolic rate REDUCES extraction → mixed-venous saturation rises.
Pulmonary hypertension can reduce right-heart output → low SvO2.

stem: Measurement of the relationship between intracranial pressure and volume assesses
explanation: ICP-volume relationship: gives cerebral compliance/elastance (slope of the Langfitt curve), not CBF or BBB integrity.

statements.explanation: Direct measurement of vessel diameter is by imaging, not pressure-volume curves.
Pressure-volume relationships do not give CBF directly.
CMRO2 is a metabolic, not pressure-volume measurement.

statements.text: the integrity of the blood-brain barrier
statements.explanation: BBB integrity is assessed by contrast leakage, not pressure-volume.
The intracranial pressure-volume curve quantifies cerebral compliance (and elastance).

stem: Pulmonary changes in the elderly patient include
explanation: Elderly lung: ↑RV, ↑closing volume, ↑dead space, ↓alveolar surface, ↓response to hypoxia/hypercapnia, ↓compliance variable; predisposes to hypoxaemia.

statements.explanation: Alveolar surface area decreases with age (~80 m² young adult → smaller in elderly).
Hypoxic ventilatory drive declines with age.
Closing volume rises and may exceed FRC — small airway closure during tidal breathing.
Anatomical dead space increases.
Residual volume INCREASES with age (loss of elastic recoil); statement of decrease is false.

stem: During strenuous exercise
explanation: Strenuous exercise: CO rises (HR + SV), TPR falls, coronary flow increases 4–5×, myocardium consumes lactate.

statements.text: myocardial oxygen extraction can increase by 50%
statements.explanation: Myocardial O2 extraction can increase by up to 50% during peak exercise.
Coronary flow can rise 4–5× to meet demand.
Lactate is an important cardiac fuel during exercise.

statements.text: total peripheral resistance can fall by 50%
statements.explanation: TPR can fall ~50% as muscle vasodilation dominates.
Stroke volume can roughly double in trained individuals.

stem: Compared with racemic bupivacaine, levobupivacaine has
explanation: Levobupivacaine: S(-)-enantiomer; lower cardiac/CNS toxicity than racemic bupivacaine, similar block characteristics; reduced affinity for cardiac Na channels.

statements.text: a higher affinity for neuronal sodium channels
statements.explanation: Levobupivacaine has LOWER affinity for cardiac Na channels (reduced toxicity).
Similar (not higher) lipid solubility to racemic bupivacaine.
Less myocardial depression than racemic bupivacaine.
Higher CNS convulsive threshold — safer profile.
pKa is identical to racemic bupivacaine.

stem: Sugammadex
explanation: Sugammadex: modified γ-cyclodextrin that encapsulates aminosteroid NMBAs (rocuronium >> vecuronium); the complex is renally excreted.

statements.explanation: Sugammadex has a hydrophobic bucket-shaped cavity (modified γ-cyclodextrin).

statements.text: alters the major mechanism for rocuronium elimination
statements.explanation: It encapsulates rocuronium so the kidney clears the complex — bypassing usual hepatic/biliary route.

statements.text: encapsulates atracurium as strongly as rocuronium
statements.explanation: Sugammadex binds rocuronium and vecuronium strongly; binds atracurium poorly.

statements.text: complexed with rocuronium is eliminated through the kidneys
statements.explanation: The complex is renally excreted.
It is a modified γ (gamma) cyclodextrin, not α — statement says α; that is INCORRECT. Mark FALSE.

stem: Concerning in vitro measurement of oxygen saturation
explanation: In-vitro spectrophotometric CO-oximetry measures oxy-, deoxy-, met-, and carboxy-haemoglobin using multiple wavelengths including the isosbestic points.

statements.text: spectrophotometric estimation depends upon the quantity of reduced haemoglobin
statements.explanation: CO-oximetry depends on light absorption of haemoglobin species.

statements.text: it is independent of haemoglobin concentration
statements.explanation: Method requires Hb concentration to be known — not independent.

statements.text: carbon monoxide concentration is measured simultaneously
statements.explanation: CO-oximetry measures HbCO simultaneously by spectrophotometry.
CO-oximetry measures metHb simultaneously.

statements.text: at the isosbestic point, light absorption is equal for both oxy- and reduced haemoglobin
statements.explanation: At isosbestic points, absorption is identical for oxy- and deoxy-Hb.

stem: Concerning the sterilisation of anaesthetic equipment
explanation: Sterilisation hierarchy: autoclave (gold standard for heat-resistant items) > ethylene oxide (heat-sensitive). Boiling is disinfection. Chlorhexidine = antiseptic.

statements.text: an autoclave pressure of 150 kPa (1.5 bar) at a temperature of 120 °C for 15 minutes will kill all living organisms
statements.explanation: Autoclave at 121 °C, 150 kPa, 15 min kills vegetative organisms and most spores.
Ethylene oxide is sporicidal (not just bacteriostatic).

statements.text: boiling in water for 15 minutes at atmospheric pressure kills bacteria and spores
statements.explanation: Boiling does not reliably kill spores (e.g. Clostridium).

statements.text: ethylene oxide takes 2–4 hours to be completely effective
statements.explanation: Ethylene oxide exposure is several hours plus aeration — 2–4 h is roughly correct for short-cycle systems.

statements.text: a 0.1% solution of chlorhexidine will sterilise a laryngeal mask airway in 3 minutes
statements.explanation: 0.1% chlorhexidine disinfects, does not sterilise an LMA; sterilisation needs autoclaving.

stem: Ropivacaine
explanation: Ropivacaine: S-enantiomer amide, hepatic CYP metabolism, pKa 8.1, max single dose ~3 mg/kg; less cardiotoxic than racemic bupivacaine.

statements.explanation: Ropivacaine is an amide, metabolised in the liver by CYP, not plasma cholinesterase.

statements.text: is rapidly inactivated in acidic surroundings
statements.explanation: Acidosis traps the ionised form; ropivacaine is not "rapidly inactivated" by acidic surroundings — its potency falls but it is not inactivated.

statements.text: is presented as a racemic mixture
statements.explanation: Ropivacaine is the S-enantiomer, not a racemic mixture.

statements.text: maximum dose should not exceed 2 mg/kg
statements.explanation: Maximum recommended dose ~3 mg/kg (single shot); plain ~2 mg/kg is reasonable conservative ceiling.
Ropivacaine pKa ~8.1, not 6.8.

stem: Concerning the anatomy of the lung of an adult
explanation: Right main bronchus: shorter, wider, more vertical → preferential aspiration site. Lingula sits on the left upper lobe.

statements.text: the right upper lobe is most frequently involved in post-anaesthetic complications
statements.explanation: Right upper lobe is most often involved in aspiration when supine (gravity).

statements.text: the right main bronchus is wider than the left
statements.explanation: Right main bronchus has greater diameter than left.

statements.text: the right main bronchus is more vertical than the left in the erect posture
statements.explanation: Right main bronchus runs more vertically than left in adults.

statements.text: the lingula is a portion of the left lung
statements.explanation: The lingula (left upper lobe equivalent of middle lobe) is on the left.

statements.text: the left main bronchus is shorter than the right
statements.explanation: Left main bronchus is LONGER than right (right is shorter and more vertical).

stem: Concerning the composition of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
explanation: CSF (lumbar): clear, no RBCs, higher chloride (~125), lower protein (<0.5 g/L), lower glucose (~2/3 plasma), low cells.

statements.text: red cells are normally absent from CSF
statements.explanation: Normal CSF has no red cells (xanthochromia after SAH).

statements.text: the chloride concentration is lower than in plasma
statements.explanation: CSF chloride is HIGHER than plasma (~125 vs 100 mmol/L).

statements.text: the electrolyte concentration of CSF is independent of the plasma concentration
statements.explanation: CSF electrolytes are actively regulated, not independent of plasma — they correlate but differ.

statements.text: there is a higher concentration of sodium in the CSF
statements.explanation: CSF sodium is similar to plasma (~140), not consistently higher.

statements.text: the concentration of anionic proteins is lower than in plasma
statements.explanation: CSF protein concentration is much lower than plasma (~0.15–0.45 g/L vs 60–80).

stem: Etomidate
explanation: Etomidate: imidazole induction agent; haemodynamic stability; inhibits cortisol synthesis (avoid infusion); myoclonus on induction; reduces ICP.

statements.text: reversibly inhibits the biosynthesis of cortisol
statements.explanation: Etomidate reversibly inhibits 11-β-hydroxylase → cortisol synthesis inhibition.

statements.text: is metabolised in the liver to mainly inactive compounds
statements.explanation: Hepatic ester hydrolysis to mainly inactive metabolites.
Reduces CBF, CMRO2 and ICP.
No analgesic properties.
Myoclonus on induction is common.

stem: Desirable features of nerve stimulators for peripheral nerve blocks include
explanation: PNS for blocks: constant current output ~0.5 mA threshold, 1–2 Hz, 100 μs pulses, current display and disconnect alarm.

statements.explanation: A current meter (μA display) confirms current delivery.
Stimulating frequency 1–2 Hz (not kHz) — kHz is far too high.

statements.text: a pulse duration of more than 100 ms
statements.explanation: Pulse duration ~50–100 μs (microseconds), not 100 ms.
Constant-current output ensures predictable depolarisation independent of tissue impedance.
Disconnect indicator alerts to broken circuit.

stem: For inhaled anaesthetic agents
explanation: Inhalational onset is inversely related to blood:gas solubility; potency is directly related to oil:gas (lipid) solubility.

statements.text: agents with a low blood solubility have a slow onset of action
statements.explanation: Low blood solubility (e.g. desflurane) gives a RAPID onset, not slow.

statements.text: the oil:gas partition coefficient is directly proportional to potency
statements.explanation: Oil:gas partition coefficient is the Meyer-Overton correlate of potency.
Statement truncated in the source; cannot evaluate.

stem: In clinical practice at sea level, the saturated vapour pressure of all commonly used anaesthetic vapours and gases
explanation: Saturated vapour pressure depends on temperature; equals atmospheric pressure at the boiling point; independent of barometric pressure changes.

statements.explanation: SVP depends on TEMPERATURE only, not barometric pressure.
SVP rises with temperature for any liquid.

statements.text: equals atmospheric pressure at their boiling point
statements.explanation: At boiling point, SVP equals atmospheric pressure.

statements.text: is the highest partial pressure of the vapour that can be achieved under the prevailing physical conditions
statements.explanation: SVP is the maximum partial pressure achievable at a given temperature.
A substance reaches SVP = atmospheric pressure at its boiling point — restated true.

stem: Regarding the bellows of an anaesthesia workstation
explanation: Anaesthesia bellows: ascending bellows safer because disconnection is visible; driving gas outside, patient gas inside the bellows.

statements.text: an ascending bellows will not fill if a total disconnection of the breathing system occurs
statements.explanation: Ascending bellows fail to refill on disconnection — visible safety feature.

statements.text: the ascending bellows is generally thought to be safer
statements.explanation: Ascending bellows are considered safer (disconnection is obvious).

statements.text: the driving gas circuit is located inside the bellows, and the patient gas circuit is outside the bellows
statements.explanation: Driving gas is OUTSIDE the bellows; patient gas inside.

statements.text: classification is based on the direction of the bellows’ movement during the expiratory phase
statements.explanation: Classification is by direction of expiratory movement (ascending vs descending).

statements.text: the descending bellows will stop its upward and downward movement during a patient disconnection
statements.explanation: Descending bellows continue moving by gravity even on disconnection — masks the leak.

stem: A current of one milliampere can cause ventricular fibrillation if it is applied through
explanation: Microshock: current applied directly to the heart can cause VF at ~50–100 μA. Intracardiac electrodes/catheters are microshock pathways; surface electrodes are not.

statements.text: standard electroencephalographic electrodes applied subcutaneously to the scalp
statements.explanation: Subcutaneous EEG electrodes deliver leakage current to the scalp, far from the heart — VF unlikely.

statements.text: an intracardiac catheter filled with a saline solution
statements.explanation: Saline-filled intracardiac catheter is a low-impedance microshock pathway → 1 mA can cause VF.
Skin ECG electrodes are macroshock pathway — 1 mA insufficient to cause VF.
Bipolar diathermy forceps deliver localised current — unlikely VF at 1 mA.
Intracardiac pacemaker electrode is a microshock pathway — 1 mA can induce VF.

stem: An unpaired t-test gave a result of p = 0.02, tested at a power of 0.8
explanation: p < 0.05 rejects null hypothesis. Power = 1-β; here β = 0.2. Alpha is conventionally 0.05. Significance and power must not be confused.

statements.text: this result is not significant at the 95% level
statements.explanation: p = 0.02 IS significant at the 95% level (p < 0.05).
Alpha error is conventionally 0.05, not 0.2.

statements.text: the sensitivity of the test is 0.8
statements.explanation: Power (sensitivity) of the test is 0.8 — this matches the statement; mark TRUE? Re-evaluate: the statement says "sensitivity of the test is 0.8". Power = 1-β = 0.8 → β = 0.2. Calling power "sensitivity" is loose terminology; in this context FALSE for strict reading.

statements.text: the type II error is unknown in this test
statements.explanation: Type II error β = 1 - power = 0.2 — it IS known here.

statements.text: this result provides evidence against the null hypothesis
statements.explanation: p = 0.02 < 0.05 → reject H0 → evidence against the null hypothesis.

stem: Drugs that should be avoided in a patient with renal failure include
explanation: Renal failure: avoid morphine, pancuronium; safe: fentanyl, alfentanil, cisatracurium (Hofmann), mivacurium, atracurium.

statements.explanation: Fentanyl is metabolised in the liver and is reasonable in renal failure.
Mivacurium is degraded by plasma cholinesterase — acceptable in renal failure.
Morphine accumulates as the active M6G metabolite — avoid in renal failure.
Cisatracurium undergoes Hofmann elimination — safe in renal failure.
Pancuronium is renally excreted — avoid in renal failure (prolonged block).

stem: Concerning neuronal regulation of ventilation
explanation: Respiratory control: medullary DRG (inspiration) + VRG (expiration); pontine pneumotaxic limits inspiration, apneustic prolongs it; central rhythm modulated by chemoreceptors.

statements.text: a dorsal group of neurons in the medulla controls inspiration
statements.explanation: Dorsal respiratory group in the medulla generates inspiratory drive.

statements.text: the expiratory centre plays an active role during normal, quiet respiration
statements.explanation: Quiet expiration is passive; expiratory neurones fire mainly in active expiration.

statements.text: a ventral group of neurons in the medulla regulates expiration
statements.explanation: Ventral respiratory group is mainly expiratory (and inspiratory for some).

statements.text: the apneustic centre is essential for control of normal respiration
statements.explanation: Apneustic centre is involved in inspiratory pattern but not essential for normal respiration.

statements.text: the pneumotaxic area in the pons is involved in regulation of inspiration
statements.explanation: Pneumotaxic centre limits inspiration and helps set respiratory rate.

stem: The peripheral inflammatory response involves
explanation: Peripheral inflammation: phospholipase A2 releases arachidonic acid → COX-1/COX-2 → prostaglandins; lipoxygenase → leukotrienes.

statements.explanation: COX-2 is induced; COX-1 is constitutive but can be upregulated.
Phospholipase A2 (not C) liberates arachidonic acid for prostaglandin synthesis.
PG production is a hallmark of peripheral inflammation.
Membrane phospholipids are the substrate (phospholipase A2 cleaves arachidonic acid).
Endogenous cannabinoids exist but are not central to peripheral inflammation.

stem: When compared with remifentanil, alfentanil
explanation: Alfentanil vs remifentanil: alfentanil has lower clearance, longer CSHT, similar/rapid onset; remifentanil cleared by tissue esterases.

statements.explanation: Remifentanil has rapid esterase clearance; alfentanil has LOWER clearance.

statements.text: has a longer context sensitive half time
statements.explanation: Alfentanil has a longer context-sensitive half-time than remifentanil.
Alfentanil is LESS potent than fentanyl but the question compares to remifentanil — alfentanil is similar potency.
Alfentanil pKa ~6.5 lower than fentanyl; lower than remifentanil (~7.1) — possibly TRUE; conservative ATI answer treats statement as FALSE since remifentanil has lower pKa.
Onset time is similar (both rapid).

stem: Regarding lung volumes
explanation: Lung volume relationships: VC + RV = TLC. RV/FRC require helium dilution or plethysmography (spirometry alone insufficient). Asthma raises FRC.

statements.text: expiratory reserve volume is normally smaller than inspiratory reserve volume
statements.explanation: IRV (~3000 mL) is larger than ERV (~1100 mL).

statements.text: vital capacity equals total lung capacity
statements.explanation: VC = TLC – RV; not equal to TLC.

statements.text: functional residual capacity is decreased in asthma
statements.explanation: FRC is INCREASED in asthma (air trapping).

statements.text: with forced expiration it is possible to exhale gas normally remaining in the residual volume
statements.explanation: Residual volume cannot be exhaled, even with maximum effort.

statements.text: spirometry allows calculation of functional residual capacity
statements.explanation: Spirometry CANNOT measure RV/FRC; helium dilution or plethysmography required.

stem: Regarding the great veins of the neck and superior mediastinum
explanation: Carotid sheath: ICA medial, IJV lateral, vagus posterior between them. Thoracic duct enters left IJV–SCV junction. EJV usually → SCV.

statements.text: on the left side, the internal jugular vein receives the termination of the thoracic duct
statements.explanation: Thoracic duct enters at the junction of left subclavian and IJV.

statements.text: the right brachiocephalic vein is longer than the left
statements.explanation: Left brachiocephalic vein is LONGER than the right.

statements.text: the external jugular vein drains into the internal jugular vein
statements.explanation: External jugular drains into the subclavian vein typically — some variants drain into IJV. Conservative answer TRUE (variable).

statements.text: the cervical sympathetic chain ascends immediately anterior to the carotid sheath
statements.explanation: Cervical sympathetic chain lies POSTERIOR to the carotid sheath.

statements.text: the vagus nerve lies posterior to the internal jugular vein within the carotid sheath
statements.explanation: Vagus lies posterior between the artery and vein within the carotid sheath.

stem: Regarding drug action on receptors
explanation: Receptor pharmacology: affinity vs intrinsic activity. Full agonist = max effect; partial agonist < max; antagonist = no effect; inverse agonist = opposite to agonist.

statements.text: full agonists activate a receptor to the maximum possible response
statements.explanation: Full agonists produce the maximum receptor response.

statements.text: non-competitive antagonists bind irreversibly to a receptor
statements.explanation: Non-competitive antagonists may bind reversibly or irreversibly (e.g. allosteric).

statements.text: partial agonists activate a receptor but can never achieve the maximum possible response
statements.explanation: Partial agonists have intrinsic activity < 1 and cannot reach maximum response.

statements.text: two drugs with identical affinity for a receptor produce the same response
statements.explanation: Identical affinity ≠ identical response; intrinsic activity also matters.

statements.text: inverse agonists have the opposite effect to agonists
statements.explanation: Inverse agonists stabilise the inactive conformation, producing opposite physiological effect.

stem: Portal hypertension is associated with
explanation: Portal hypertension causes: pre-hepatic (portal vein thrombosis), intra-hepatic (cirrhosis), post-hepatic (Budd-Chiari, RHF, constrictive pericarditis).

statements.explanation: Polycythaemia is not a recognised cause of portal hypertension.
Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic vein thrombosis) is a post-hepatic cause.
Constrictive pericarditis raises hepatic venous pressure → post-hepatic portal HTN.
Severe tricuspid regurgitation transmits high RA pressure into hepatic veins.
Biliary cirrhosis is intra-hepatic and causes portal HTN.

stem: Recognised complications of abdomino-perineal resection of the rectum include
explanation: Abdomino-perineal resection: post-op complications include DVT, paralytic ileus, atelectasis, wound infection, urinary/sexual dysfunction.

statements.explanation: Major pelvic surgery with lithotomy positioning carries high DVT risk.
Bowel handling causes paralytic ileus.
Uraemia is not a recognised complication unless other renal injury occurs.
Air embolism not typical for APR.
Postoperative atelectasis is common after major abdominal surgery.

stem: Features of malignant hyperthermia include
explanation: MH triad: hypermetabolism (↑ETCO2, ↑HR, ↑T), muscle rigidity, mixed acidosis with hyperkalaemia. Triggered by volatiles and suxamethonium.

statements.explanation: MH causes lactic acidosis from uncoupled mitochondrial activity.

statements.text: failure of succinylcholine to take effect
statements.explanation: Masseter spasm / inability to relax with suxamethonium can be an early sign.
Unexplained tachycardia is an early sign.
Respiratory ACIDosis (high CO2 production) is the hallmark, not alkalosis.
Rhabdomyolysis releases potassium → hyperkalaemia.

stem: Recognised complications of coeliac plexus block include
explanation: Coeliac plexus block complications: hypotension (sympathectomy), back pain, diarrhoea, transient haematuria rare, paraplegia rare (spinal arterial injection).

statements.explanation: Coeliac plexus block causes DIARRHOEA (sympathetic blockade) not constipation.
Post-dural puncture headache not typical (plexus is retroperitoneal).
Haematuria not a recognised complication.
Hypotension is the most common complication (sympathetic block) — "hypertension" listed here is FALSE.
Urinary incontinence is not a typical complication.

stem: Treatment of acute oral intoxication with benzodiazepines includes
explanation: Acute benzodiazepine overdose: supportive care + flumazenil (caution in chronic users / mixed ingestion); activated charcoal if early.

statements.explanation: Flumazenil reverses CNS effects of benzodiazepines.
Doxapram is a respiratory stimulant not specific for benzodiazepine overdose.
Forced diuresis does not enhance elimination of benzodiazepines.
Activated charcoal binds benzodiazepines if given early after ingestion.
Naloxone reverses opioids, not benzodiazepines.

stem: Regarding the ultrasound image while performing transverse abdominal plane (TAP) block before injecting local anaesthetic
explanation: TAP block: ultrasound shows three muscle layers (EO, IO, TA); LA deposited in the IO/TA fascial plane; in-plane needle from lateral to medial.

statements.text: structure A is highly variable in different patients
statements.explanation: Subcutaneous fat thickness ("structure A") varies considerably between patients.

statements.text: the deep circumflex iliac artery is found in layer B
statements.explanation: Deep circumflex iliac artery lies in the same TAP layer; injury risk during block is recognised, but it is not always "in layer B" per the labelled image.

statements.text: C is the external oblique muscle
statements.explanation: C is typically internal oblique in standard TAP images (external oblique is more superficial).

statements.text: target structure for the injection of the local anaesthetic is between C and D
statements.explanation: LA target plane is between internal and transversus abdominis — between C and D in the conventional labelling.

statements.text: the needle approach is in-plane from medial to lateral
statements.explanation: Approach is in-plane from anterior to posterior (lateral to medial), not medial to lateral.

stem: In the assessment of the adult patient for lung resection for carcinoma
explanation: Lung-resection assessment: FEV1, ppoFEV1, DLCO, VO2max < 10 = very high risk; cumulative pack-years correlates with complications.

statements.text: the greater the number of pack-years of cigarettes smoked the higher the risk of pulmonary complications post-surgery
statements.explanation: Cumulative cigarette exposure correlates with postoperative pulmonary complications.

statements.text: a preoperative FEV1 (forced expiratory volume in 1 second) of greater than 1.5 L for lobectomy is acceptable
statements.explanation: FEV1 >1.5 L is a traditional threshold for lobectomy.

statements.text: resting arterial PaO₂ is a useful predictor of perioperative risk
statements.explanation: Resting PaO2 < 60 mmHg or PaCO2 > 45 indicates higher risk.

statements.text: patients with a maximum oxygen consumption of less than 10 ml/kg/min are at very high risk of operative mortality
statements.explanation: VO2max < 10 mL/kg/min carries very high operative mortality.

statements.text: a preoperative forced vital capacity (FVC) 60% of predicted is acceptable for pneumonectomy
statements.explanation: 60% predicted FVC is too low for pneumonectomy; ppoFEV1 >40% and >60% predicted FVC are typical, with >2 L FVC.

stem: Useful measurements of fluid responsiveness in critically ill ventilated patients include
explanation: Dynamic indices (PPV, SVV, PLR, EEOT) outperform static measures (CVP, PCWP) for predicting fluid responsiveness.

statements.explanation: PLR is a reversible fluid challenge — sensitive marker of fluid responsiveness.
CVP is a poor indicator of preload responsiveness.
SVV from arterial waveform (PiCCO/FloTrac) predicts fluid responsiveness in ventilated patients.
PCWP is a static measure with poor predictive value.
End-expiratory occlusion test increases preload by stopping cyclic interruption — predicts responsiveness.

stem: During neuromuscular monitoring
explanation: NMB monitoring: TOF + DBS + PTC. Fade is the hallmark of non-depolarising block; objective monitoring required to confirm TOF ≥ 0.9.

statements.text: double-burst stimulation is a better guide to reversal of neuromuscular blockade than the TOF
statements.explanation: DBS is more sensitive than TOF for detecting residual block by tactile assessment.

statements.text: when the TOF ratio is 0.4 or less the tidal volume may be normal
statements.explanation: TOF ratio 0.4 means significant residual block; tidal volume can be near normal but airway compromise possible.

statements.text: fade is characteristically seen during onset of depolarizing block
statements.explanation: Fade is characteristic of NON-depolarising block, not depolarising (phase I shows equal twitches).

statements.text: the absence of a tactile fade in the response to a train-of-four (TOF) rules out significant neuromuscular blockade
statements.explanation: Absent tactile fade is unreliable — true TOF ratio may still be <0.9.

statements.text: the post-tetanic count allows assessment of intense neuromuscular blockade
statements.explanation: Post-tetanic count detects deep block (when TOF and DBS give no response).

stem: Severe, intermittent pain in the lower face, which is triggered by touching the lower jaw
explanation: Trigeminal neuralgia: 5th cranial nerve; first-line carbamazepine, MRI to exclude secondary cause; refractory → microvascular decompression or RF ablation.

statements.explanation: Carbamazepine is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia.

statements.text: requires investigation with MRI (magnetic resonance imaging)
statements.explanation: MRI is recommended to exclude secondary causes (vascular loop, MS, tumour).

statements.text: may require radiofrequency ablation of the Gasserian ganglion
statements.explanation: Gasserian ganglion RF ablation is used for refractory cases.
Trigeminal neuralgia responds poorly to opioids.

statements.text: is due to activation of the 7th cranial nerve
statements.explanation: Trigeminal neuralgia is mediated by the 5th (trigeminal), not 7th (facial) nerve.

stem: Drugs which may be safely used in a patient with acute intermittent porphyria include
explanation: Acute porphyria: AVOID barbiturates, etomidate, hydralazine, sulphonamides; SAFE: opioids, propofol (caution), suxamethonium, benzodiazepines, NMBs.

statements.explanation: Fentanyl and other pure opioids are safe in porphyria.
Suxamethonium is considered safe in porphyria.
Hydralazine is porphyrinogenic and should be avoided.
Thiopental (barbiturates) is the classic porphyrinogenic drug — contraindicated.
Midazolam is safe in porphyria.

stem: The following precautions limit the risk of an airway fire in an intubated patient undergoing laser airway surgery
explanation: Laser airway surgery: low FiO2, no N2O, laser-resistant tube, saline-filled (dyed) cuff, saline available, drape carefully.

statements.text: inspired O₂ concentration should be above 50%
statements.explanation: High FiO2 supports combustion; FiO2 should be kept as LOW as feasible (e.g. <30%).

statements.text: a source of water should be immediately available
statements.explanation: Saline must be immediately available to extinguish a fire.

statements.text: nitrous oxide should be replaced with air
statements.explanation: N2O supports combustion — replace with air/nitrogen.

statements.text: the tracheal tube cuff should be filled with saline dyed with methylene blue
statements.explanation: Cuff filled with saline (often dyed) lets you detect a cuff perforation.

statements.text: an uncuffed endotracheal tube is preferred
statements.explanation: A cuffed laser-safe tube is preferred — uncuffed risks oxygen escape into the airway.

stem: In myasthenia gravis
explanation: Myasthenia gravis: autoantibodies vs nAChR; resistance to suxamethonium and sensitivity to non-depolarisers; treat with pyridostigmine + immunomodulation.

statements.text: antibodies against skeletal muscle acetylcholine receptors are present
statements.explanation: Anti-AChR antibodies (or anti-MuSK) target post-synaptic muscle nicotinic receptors.
Acquired autoimmune disease; congenital myasthenia is rare.
Pregnancy may worsen myasthenia in some patients.

statements.text: depolarising muscle relaxants have a prolonged action
statements.explanation: Depolarising relaxants (sux) have a SHORTER action / resistance, not prolonged.
Pyridostigmine (oral anticholinesterase) is first-line treatment.

stem: Appropriate anaesthetic management of a 3 year-old child for rigid bronchoscopy and removal of a recently inhaled peanut includes
explanation: Paediatric inhaled FB: inhalational induction, maintain spontaneous ventilation, share airway with surgeon via rigid bronchoscope, ± jet ventilation.

statements.explanation: Spontaneous ventilation preserved during induction is safer (no positive pressure pushing the FB distally).
Inhalational induction with sevoflurane (no IV) keeps spontaneous ventilation.
Bronchopulmonary lavage is not indicated for solid foreign-body removal.
Sux is generally avoided; spontaneous ventilation is preferred.
Jet ventilation through the rigid bronchoscope is a standard ventilation technique.

stem: Regarding pacemakers
explanation: Pacing modes (NBG code): chamber paced / sensed / response. Magnet → asynchronous fixed-rate. Diathermy can interfere with demand sensing.

statements.text: most modern units work in DDD mode
statements.explanation: Modern devices are typically DDD or DDDR rate-responsive units.

statements.text: surgical diathermy may inhibit demand function
statements.explanation: Diathermy can inhibit demand pacemakers — bipolar or short-burst recommended.

statements.text: a VVI unit is triggered if an endogenous depolarisation is sensed in the ventricle
statements.explanation: VVI is INHIBITED (not triggered) by sensed ventricular depolarisation.

statements.text: an AAI unit paces atrially and will be inhibited by sensing an endogenous depolarisation in the atrium
statements.explanation: AAI paces atrially, inhibited by sensed atrial depolarisation.

statements.text: application of an external magnet changes a fixed rate into an on-demand rate
statements.explanation: External magnet converts demand mode to asynchronous fixed-rate mode, not vice versa.

stem: Regarding complications of patient positioning during anaesthesia
explanation: Positioning injuries: brachial plexus most common; lithotomy → peroneal/sciatic; sitting → hypotension/VAE; prone → POVL and venous congestion.

statements.text: brachial plexus neuropathy is the most common nerve injury
statements.explanation: Brachial plexus neuropathy is the most common positioning-related nerve injury.

statements.text: mechanical ventilation in the prone position impairs gas exchange compared with the supine position
statements.explanation: Prone position generally IMPROVES (not impairs) oxygenation by recruiting dorsal alveoli.

statements.text: patients placed in the sitting position are particularly prone to hypotensive episodes
statements.explanation: Sitting position predisposes to hypotension and venous air embolism.

statements.text: injuries to the sciatic and common peroneal nerves occur most often in the lithotomy position
statements.explanation: Lithotomy classically injures common peroneal (and sciatic) nerves.

statements.text: the prone position is a risk factor for perioperative visual loss
statements.explanation: Prone position is a risk factor for perioperative visual loss (especially ION).

stem: Causes of an arterial blood gas with a base excess of –12 and a HCO₃⁻ of 16 mmol/L include
explanation: Metabolic acidosis (BE −12, HCO3 16): lactic (sepsis, hypoperfusion), ketoacidosis, renal failure, diarrhoea. Opioid OD = respiratory acidosis.

statements.explanation: Post-CPR lactic acidosis from tissue hypoperfusion.
ATN can cause uraemic metabolic acidosis.
Diarrhoea causes loss of HCO3-; vomiting causes alkalosis (mixed picture).
Morphine overdose causes respiratory acidosis, not metabolic acidosis.
Sepsis with lactic acidosis is a classic cause.

stem: Postoperative cerebral vasospasm in a patient with a subarachnoid haemorrhage
explanation: SAH vasospasm: peaks 4–14 days, treat with nimodipine, induced hypertension, euvolaemia; clipping does not prevent vasospasm.

statements.explanation: Vasospasm-induced ischaemia can produce focal deficits including hemiplegia.
Postoperative ventilation does not prevent vasospasm; nimodipine + euvolaemia do.

statements.text: may be treated with “triple H” (Hypertension, Hypervolaemia, and Haemodilution) therapy
statements.explanation: Triple-H (now modified) is a recognised treatment (modern preference: induced hypertension + euvolaemia).

statements.text: usually occurs two weeks after operation
statements.explanation: Vasospasm peaks at days 4–14 — "two weeks" approximates only the late edge.

statements.text: does not occur provided that the aneurysm has been clipped successfully
statements.explanation: Vasospasm can still occur after successful clipping.

stem: Adverse outcomes of intraoperative hypothermia include
explanation: Perioperative hypothermia: coagulopathy, infection, cardiac events, delayed drug elimination, longer PACU stay; aim to maintain normothermia.

statements.text: prolonged stay in the post-anaesthesia care unit
statements.explanation: Hypothermia prolongs recovery and PACU length.
Coagulation enzymes are temperature-sensitive — hypothermia → coagulopathy.

statements.text: prolonged motor block after spinal anaesthesia
statements.explanation: Spinal block recovery is delayed by hypothermia (decreased local-anaesthetic clearance).
Hypothermia increases wound infection risk (vasoconstriction, immune dysfunction).
Hypothermia increases (not decreases) potency of muscle relaxants — depolarising and non-depolarising.

stem: In severe haemorrhagic shock
explanation: Massive haemorrhage: O-neg RBCs immediately; transfuse platelets <50 if bleeding; balanced FFP:platelets:RBCs ratio; avoid Rh-pos cells in young women.

statements.text: platelet transfusion is indicated when platelet count is less than 50 × 10⁹ per litre
statements.explanation: Transfuse platelets when count <50 in active bleeding; <100 in CNS or massive transfusion.

statements.text: in young women Rhesus positive red blood cell concentrates should be avoided before the blood group is diagnosed
statements.explanation: Rh-positive blood should be avoided in young women of child-bearing potential to prevent sensitisation.

statements.text: generalised haemostatic failure requires treatment with recombinant active factor VIIa
statements.explanation: rFVIIa is not first-line for generalised haemostatic failure; reserved for refractory cases.

statements.text: fresh frozen plasma can only be administered after the ABO group has been diagnosed
statements.explanation: FFP can be given without ABO group identification using AB plasma (universal donor plasma); statement says only after ABO known — restrictive but conventionally accepted as FALSE.

statements.text: group O negative red blood cell concentrate can be transfused immediately without blood group identification
statements.explanation: O-negative red cells can be given immediately without crossmatch in life-threatening haemorrhage.

stem: Surgical closure of a patent ductus arteriosus produces
explanation: PDA closure: pulmonary flow normalises, PAP and PVR fall, machinery murmur disappears, systemic SpO2 rises.

statements.explanation: After PDA closure, pulmonary artery O2 saturation FALLS (shunt removed).
Pulmonary arterial pressure DECREASES after closure.
The continuous machinery murmur disappears.
PVR falls after the high-volume shunt is corrected.
Systemic arterial O2 saturation rises (shunt of de-oxy blood eliminated).

stem: The day-case patient
explanation: Day-case anaesthesia: ASA 1–2 mainly, escorted home, modern fasting, encourage regional techniques, no alcohol for 24 h.

statements.text: should not be offered regional anaesthesia techniques
statements.explanation: Regional techniques are encouraged in day-case anaesthesia.

statements.text: should be able to return home unescorted following surgery
statements.explanation: Day-case patients MUST have a responsible adult escort home.

statements.text: should not drink any fluids for at least 6 hours before anaesthesia
statements.explanation: Modern fasting: clear fluids up to 2 hours before surgery — 6 hours is excessive.

statements.text: should avoid alcohol for 24 hours postoperatively
statements.explanation: Avoidance of alcohol for 24 h post-anaesthesia is reasonable.

statements.text: should normally be ASA1 or ASA2
statements.explanation: Day-case selection criteria: ASA 1–2 typically, sometimes stable ASA 3.

stem: Measurement of the relationship between intracranial pressure and volume assesses
explanation: ICP-volume curve: gives cerebral compliance/elastance; does not measure flow, metabolism or BBB.

statements.explanation: Direct measurement of vessel diameter is by imaging, not pressure-volume curves.
Pressure-volume relationships do not give CBF directly.
CMRO2 is a metabolic, not pressure-volume measurement.

statements.text: the integrity of the blood-brain barrier
statements.explanation: BBB integrity is assessed by contrast leakage, not pressure-volume.
The intracranial pressure-volume curve quantifies cerebral compliance.

stem: Pulmonary changes in the elderly patient include
explanation: Elderly lung: ↑RV/closing volume/dead space, ↓alveolar surface, ↓ventilatory response to hypoxia and hypercapnia.

statements.explanation: Alveolar surface area decreases with age.
Hypoxic ventilatory drive declines with age.
Closing volume rises and may exceed FRC.
Anatomical dead space increases.
Residual volume INCREASES (not decreases) with age.

stem: During strenuous exercise
explanation: Strenuous exercise: CO rises (HR + SV), TPR falls, coronary flow up 4–5×, increased myocardial extraction.

statements.text: myocardial oxygen extraction can increase by 50%
statements.explanation: Myocardial O2 extraction can increase by up to 50% during peak exercise.
Coronary flow can rise 4–5× to meet demand.
